items.title: The Haves and Have-Nots of the AI Gold Rush
items.summary: Over the past five years, approximately 10,000 employees at AI-first companies like Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, Nvidia, and Meta have accumulated retirement-level wealth above $20 million, creating a stark economic divide between AI insiders and the broader workforce. The piece examines how equity compensation at frontier AI labs has generated extraordinary wealth for a tiny cohort while the productivity gains from AI remain unevenly distributed across the wider economy.
items.whyItMatters: As AI reshapes entire industries, understanding who actually captures its economic benefits is critical for policymakers, workers, and society — the wealth gap between AI insiders and everyone else is widening at an unprecedented pace.

knowledgeChecks.question: Approximately how many tech workers have achieved retirement-level wealth (over $20M) from the AI boom, according to the TechCrunch report?
knowledgeChecks.explanation: The article estimates approximately 10,000 employees — primarily at Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, Nvidia, and Meta — have accumulated over $20 million in wealth over the past five years through equity compensation tied to the AI boom.

knowledgeChecks.question: Which companies are specifically highlighted as the primary sources of AI wealth concentration in the article?
options: Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, Nvidia, and Meta
knowledgeChecks.explanation: The article specifically names Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI, Nvidia, and Meta as the companies whose early employees have achieved the greatest concentration of wealth from the AI gold rush.

items.title: $60B AI Chip Darling Cerebras Almost Died Early On, Burning $8M a Month
items.summary: Cerebras Systems, fresh off a blockbuster IPO that valued the company near $60 billion, nearly collapsed in its early years by burning $8 million per month trying to solve a single technical problem — building its Wafer Scale Engine chips. CEO Andrew Feldman revealed the company had incinerated nearly $200 million before achieving its breakthrough, later signing landmark deals with OpenAI and AWS worth tens of billions. The story offers a rare window into the extreme capital risk behind AI hardware innovation.
items.whyItMatters: Cerebras' near-death experience illustrates the enormous capital requirements and existential risk embedded in AI hardware development — and raises questions about how many competing chip startups can survive long enough to reach profitability.

knowledgeChecks.question: How much was Cerebras burning per month during its early crisis years, according to CEO Andrew Feldman?
knowledgeChecks.explanation: CEO Andrew Feldman revealed that Cerebras was spending approximately $8 million a month and had burned through nearly $200 million in total while trying to solve the technical challenge of building its Wafer Scale Engine chip.

knowledgeChecks.question: What is the core technical product that defines Cerebras' competitive advantage in AI computing?
knowledgeChecks.explanation: Cerebras' entire value proposition rests on its Wafer Scale Engine (WSE) — a chip built from an entire silicon wafer rather than individual dies — which Cerebras claims delivers faster AI inference than traditional GPU-based systems from Nvidia.

items.title: Research Repository ArXiv Will Ban Authors for a Year If They Let AI Do All the Work
items.summary: ArXiv, the widely used open preprint repository for scientific research, is cracking down on careless use of large language models in academic papers — announcing it will ban authors for a full year if AI is found to have performed the primary intellectual work of a submission. The policy distinguishes between acceptable AI assistance (editing, grammar, literature search) and unacceptable AI authorship (AI generating the core research ideas, analysis, and writing). This marks one of the first major institutional enforcement actions against AI-generated scientific content.
items.whyItMatters: As LLMs become embedded in research workflows, ArXiv's ban sets a precedent for how academic institutions will define the line between AI assistance and AI authorship — a debate with major implications for scientific trust, reproducibility, and career incentives.

knowledgeChecks.question: What penalty will ArXiv impose on authors who allow AI to perform the primary intellectual work in their submissions?
options: A written warning and mandatory revision
A one-year ban from the platform
Retraction of the paper with no further action
knowledgeChecks.explanation: ArXiv's new policy imposes a one-year ban on authors found to have let AI generate the substantial intellectual content of their submissions, distinguishing this from acceptable uses of AI as a writing aid or editing tool.

knowledgeChecks.question: Which type of AI use does ArXiv's policy specifically prohibit?
options: Using AI to search and summarize related literature
Using AI to run statistical analyses on datasets
Using AI as a grammar checker or writing editor
Using AI to generate the core research ideas, analysis, and text of a paper
knowledgeChecks.explanation: ArXiv's policy targets submissions where AI has performed the primary intellectual work — generating the research framing, analysis, and writing — rather than serving as an assistive tool for tasks like editing, grammar correction, or literature discovery.

items.title: Cerebras' Blockbuster IPO Fuels Investor Optimism for SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic
items.summary: Cerebras Systems' explosive market debut — where shares nearly doubled on the first day, briefly pushing the AI chip company past a $100 billion valuation — has reinvigorated investor appetite for AI company listings. Wall Street is now focused on the next wave of potential AI IPOs: SpaceX is expected to file its public prospectus soon, while OpenAI and Anthropic are both eyeing public debuts later in 2026. The success signals that public markets are ready to absorb large-scale AI valuations.
items.whyItMatters: Cerebras' IPO success is a bellwether for the broader AI investment cycle — if public markets can support a $60–100B AI chip company, it dramatically increases the likelihood that OpenAI and Anthropic will accelerate their own paths to IPO.

knowledgeChecks.question: What happened to Cerebras' share price on its first day of trading?
options: It fell 20% below its IPO price
It remained flat near its IPO price
It nearly doubled, briefly pushing the company past $100 billion
knowledgeChecks.explanation: Cerebras shares nearly doubled on their first trading day, with the stock opening at approximately $350 against its $185 IPO price and briefly pushing the company's market cap past $100 billion before settling lower in subsequent sessions.

knowledgeChecks.question: Which companies' potential IPOs gained renewed investor attention following Cerebras' successful public debut?
knowledgeChecks.explanation: Cerebras' blockbuster IPO reinvigorated investor interest specifically in SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic — all of which are eyeing their own public listings in 2026 and benefited from the market's demonstrated appetite for high-valuation AI companies.
